title: Deploy a Production-Ready NVIDIA AI-Q Blueprint on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA's AI-Q 2.0 Blueprint, an open-source multi-agent research framework built on LangChain Deep Agents and the NeMo Agent Toolkit, can be deployed production-ready on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure in 20–25 minutes using Terraform and Helm. The architecture features an intent router directing queries to either a shallow research agent or a multi-step deep agent with planning and researcher sub-agents. The system's extensibility—every layer swappable via YAML—signals a maturing enterprise AI agent stack where NVIDIA, Oracle, and LangChain are positioning for cloud-native agentic workloads.

themes.theme: NVIDIA–Oracle Cloud Partnership for Agentic AI

insights.claim: NVIDIA and Oracle have jointly released a production-ready AI-Q 2.0 Blueprint deployable on OCI, signaling a deepening partnership to capture enterprise agentic AI workloads on Oracle's cloud infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: NVIDIA is embedding its AI agent stack directly into OCI, strengthening Oracle's AI differentiation and NVIDIA's software ecosystem lock-in beyond pure GPU hardware sales.
insights.quote: This post shows you how to deploy AI-Q 2.0 on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) using Terraform to create the OCI resources and Helm to install the workloads on OKE.

insights.claim: Oracle's OKE (Kubernetes Engine), OCI Vault, Load Balancer, and Block Volume services are the foundational cloud resources underpinning NVIDIA's AI-Q deployment, making OCI a full-stack AI platform rather than just IaaS.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Oracle is building credibility as an enterprise AI cloud provider by integrating with NVIDIA's agent toolkit at the infrastructure level, competing with AWS, Azure, and GCP for GPU-accelerated AI workloads.
insights.quote: Terraform creates the VCN, OKE cluster, Load Balancer, and the Vault with the NGC and Tavily API keys encrypted at rest.

insights.claim: Sanjay Basu, Oracle's senior director of solutions architecture for generative AI, co-authored the NVIDIA deployment guide, confirming executive-level Oracle commitment to NVIDIA AI-Q integration.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Cross-company senior engineering collaboration on published reference architectures is a leading indicator of deeper commercial agreements and joint go-to-market efforts.
insights.quote: Sanjay Basu is a senior director of solutions architecture, leading the generative AI and GPU cloud engineering teams at Oracle Cloud.

insights.claim: AI agent architectures have evolved from single-turn Q&A to long-horizon multi-agent systems with planning sub-agents, researcher sub-agents, sandboxed tool execution, and shared filesystem layers—a progression NVIDIA has codified in the AI-Q 2.0 Blueprint.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Enterprises adopting AI agents are no longer evaluating prototypes but production-grade multi-agent orchestration; vendors offering complete stacks (NVIDIA, LangChain) gain over point solutions.
insights.quote: Today, we have long-horizon agents. Systems that plan many steps, split work between sub-agents, keep context across a long task, and run tools in a safe sandbox.

insights.claim: The AI-Q Blueprint's intent router dynamically routes queries to either a shallow research agent (fast, bounded tool-augmented search) or a deep agent (multi-step planning and researcher sub-agents), enabling cost-efficient tiered inference.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Tiered routing within a single agent framework reduces unnecessary deep-compute invocations, lowering inference cost—a key enterprise selling point and a model for efficient agentic system design.
insights.quote: The intent router reads each user query and sends it to the right workflow.

insights.claim: The AI-Q Blueprint uses FastAPI for the backend agent server, Next.js for the frontend, and PostgreSQL for job store and checkpoints—a conventional web stack adapted for agentic AI, lowering the adoption barrier for enterprise developers.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Standardizing AI agent infrastructure on familiar web stack components (FastAPI, Postgres) accelerates enterprise adoption without requiring specialized AI infrastructure expertise.
insights.quote: Backend (aiq-backend): A FastAPI-based agent server that runs the AI-Q workflow. Frontend (aiq-frontend): A next.js web UI exposed over NodePort 30080. PostgreSQL (aiq-postgres): An in-cluster database for the job store, checkpoints, and summaries.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's NeMo Agent Toolkit plugin system allows swapping LLMs, sub-agents, and RAG backends via YAML configuration alone—eliminating code rewrites and positioning NVIDIA as a model-agnostic agent orchestration layer.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Model-agnostic orchestration frameworks reduce switching costs for enterprises but increase dependency on the orchestration layer itself (NVIDIA NeMo), creating a new form of vendor lock-in.
insights.quote: Swapping an LLM, adding a sub-agent, or plugging a new RAG backend is a configuration change, not a rewrite.

themes.theme: NVIDIA NGC as Enterprise AI Distribution Channel

insights.claim: NVIDIA distributes AI-Q 2.0 via NGC (NVIDIA GPU Cloud) as a pre-packaged Helm chart and container images, establishing NGC as the enterprise distribution channel for production AI agent stacks—analogous to an app store for GPU-accelerated workloads.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: NGC adoption as the primary distribution channel for AI blueprints creates a software moat for NVIDIA: enterprises dependent on NGC for pre-built AI workloads are implicitly tied to NVIDIA infrastructure and API keys.
insights.quote: The chart and container images are published on NGC, so there's nothing to build locally. We point kubectl at the new OKE cluster, create the secrets the chart consumes, then helm pull and helm install.

insights.claim: The NGC API key (nvapi-...) serves dual purpose as both the inference authentication key and the container registry pull credential for nvcr.io, tightly coupling NVIDIA's cloud inference services with its software distribution infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Single-key authentication across inference and registry creates strong platform stickiness—enterprises managing one NVIDIA credential for all AI workloads are less likely to fragment to competing providers.
insights.quote: The NGC key does double duty. It's both the inference key and the nvcr.io pull credential.

insights.claim: NVIDIA routes AI inference for its AI-Q Blueprint through build.nvidia.com, its cloud-hosted inference endpoint, meaning even self-hosted OCI deployments generate ongoing inference API revenue for NVIDIA.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: NVIDIA captures software/API revenue from enterprises deploying 'self-hosted' AI agents—a classic razor-and-blades model extending beyond GPU hardware to cloud inference services.
insights.quote: nvidia_api_key, your NVIDIA NGC key from build.nvidia.com. Used for inference and to pull container images from nvcr.io.

themes.theme: LangChain and Tavily as Infrastructure Layer for Enterprise Agents

insights.claim: LangChain Deep Agents is embedded as a core dependency in NVIDIA's AI-Q 2.0 Blueprint, cementing LangChain's position as foundational orchestration middleware in enterprise production-grade AI agent deployments.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: LangChain gaining inclusion in NVIDIA's official production blueprints validates its transition from developer tool to enterprise infrastructure—relevant for assessing LangChain's competitive moat against AWS Bedrock Agents and similar native orchestrators.
insights.quote: It is built on LangChain Deep Agents and the NVIDIA NeMo Agent Toolkit.

insights.claim: Tavily (a web search API service) is a required external dependency for NVIDIA's AI-Q Blueprint, indicating that real-time web search capability is now considered a baseline requirement for production AI agent deployments.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Tavily's inclusion in a major vendor's reference architecture validates real-time search-augmented generation as table stakes for enterprise AI agents, and signals commercial traction for specialized search API providers.
insights.quote: Tavily API key from tavily.com, format tvly-…

themes.theme: Infrastructure-as-Code Approach to AI Agent Deployment

insights.claim: NVIDIA's AI-Q 2.0 Blueprint uses Terraform for OCI infrastructure provisioning and Helm for Kubernetes workload deployment, establishing infrastructure-as-code as the standard deployment pattern for enterprise AI agent systems.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: DevOps tooling (Terraform/Helm) becoming the standard interface for AI agent deployment lowers barriers for platform engineering teams and favors cloud-native providers with strong IaC ecosystems.
insights.quote: The deployment uses Terraform for the OCI resources and Helm for the Kubernetes workloads. This gives a clean split between infrastructure and application, and one terraform destroy is enough to remove everything later.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's AI-Q deployment on OCI can be fully provisioned in 20–25 minutes and torn down with a single 'terraform destroy' command, reducing the barrier for enterprises to experiment with production-grade AI agents at cloud scale.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Sub-30-minute provisioning times for production AI agent infrastructure signal that cloud-native AI deployment has reached commodity status, accelerating enterprise adoption cycles.
insights.quote: Total time: around 20 to 25 minutes.

insights.claim: OCI Vault provides AES-256 encrypted storage for API keys used in the AI-Q Blueprint, while Kubernetes secrets serve as the runtime credential delivery mechanism—a two-layer secrets architecture that separates audit/DR concerns from operational access.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Enterprise security architecture for AI agents is converging on cloud-native secrets management (Vault) plus Kubernetes secrets, creating opportunities for security tooling vendors targeting AI workload compliance.
insights.quote: Terraform stores the NGC and Tavily keys in OCI Vault at provision time (for audit and disaster recovery), but the running pods read them from the aiq-credentials Kubernetes secret you created in Step 3c.

themes.theme: Cloud Cost and Operational Considerations for AI Agents

insights.claim: NVIDIA explicitly warns that OKE node pools and OCI Load Balancers continue incurring costs while idle, advising users to destroy stacks between experiments—highlighting that persistent AI agent infrastructure carries meaningful ongoing cloud spend.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Production AI agent deployments generate continuous infrastructure costs independent of usage; enterprises need FinOps disciplines for AI workloads, creating demand for cloud cost management tools targeting AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: Cost: The OKE node pool and the Load Balancer keep costing you while they run. Destroy the stack between experiments, or scale the node pool down to zero.

insights.claim: Rotating API keys in NVIDIA's AI-Q deployment requires deleting and recreating Kubernetes secrets followed by a rolling restart of deployments, as editing Terraform variables alone does not propagate to running pods—a operational complexity that scales with enterprise deployments.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Operational complexity in AI agent credential management (manual key rotation, rolling restarts) will drive demand for automated secrets rotation tooling and managed AI infrastructure services.
insights.quote: To rotate, delete and re-create that secret with the new values, then kubectl rollout restart deployment -n ns-aiq aiq-backend. Editing terraform.tfvars alone won't reach the pods.

insights.claim: NVIDIA is releasing AI-Q as open-source software with a public GitHub repository (oracle-samples/ai-q), using the open-source model to drive ecosystem adoption and position NeMo Agent Toolkit as the de facto enterprise agentic orchestration standard.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Open-source release of production-grade AI agent blueprints is NVIDIA's ecosystem play—analogous to how CUDA drove GPU adoption, NeMo/AI-Q aims to make NVIDIA infrastructure the default for enterprise AI agents.
insights.quote: The NVIDIA AI-Q Blueprint is an open source reference for this kind of agent.

insights.claim: NVIDIA is planning a multi-part blog series on AI-Q with Parts 2 and 3 covering extensibility features (swapping LLMs, adding sub-agents, plugging RAG backends), indicating a systematic ecosystem education effort targeting enterprise platform engineers.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: A structured multi-part content series signals NVIDIA's commitment to sustained developer education around AI-Q, a leading indicator of long-term product investment and growing ecosystem prioritization.
insights.quote: We will use that extensibility in Parts 2 and 3 of this series.

insights.claim: NVIDIA's AI-Q Blueprint enables both quick cited answers (shallow research agent) and deep multi-step research reports with sources (deep agent), targeting knowledge worker productivity use cases as the primary enterprise deployment scenario.
insights.speaker: Anurag Kuppala
insights.so_what: Positioning AI agents for research report generation—a high-value knowledge worker task—targets enterprises willing to pay premium per-query inference costs, supporting NVIDIA's cloud inference revenue model.
insights.quote: You can use it for quick cited answers, or for longer research reports with sources.
